conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. 

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

First, let me check the official guidance to ensure I have the most accurate information for you.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. Now I can help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit.

To start, could you please tell me: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'd like to find out if I'm eligible for Child Benefit. I have one child, Alex, who is 8 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you have one child, Alex, who is 8 years old.

To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask you a few questions:

**Do you live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. So you live in the UK in Manchester, and Alex is 8 years old.

Next question: **Does Alex live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't live with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. Since Alex doesn't live with you, I need to understand your situation a bit more.

**Do you make regular financial contributions towards Alex's upkeep (such as for food, clothes, pocket money, or towards their accommodation)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do contribute towards Alex's upkeep - I help with things like clothes, food, and other expenses.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. That's helpful to know.

**Can you tell me approximately how much you contribute towards Alex's upkeep each week or month?**

(For reference, the current Child Benefit rate is £25.60 per week for the eldest or only child, which is about £110 per month.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I contribute £30 per week towards Alex's upkeep.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. That's helpful information - you're contributing £30 per week, which is more than the Child Benefit rate of £25.60 per week for an only child.

Now I need to ask: **Who does Alex currently live with?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex lives with their other parent.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. 

One more important question: **Is the other parent currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, they're not claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me make sure I have everything correct:

- You have one child, Alex, who is 8 years old
- You live in the UK (Manchester)
- Alex lives with their other parent
- The other parent is NOT claiming Child Benefit for Alex
- You contribute £30 per week towards Alex's upkeep

Based on the official guidance I've reviewed, you can be eligible for Child Benefit even if the child doesn't live with you, as long as you're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or the equivalent in kind) towards looking after them - for example on food, clothes or pocket money.

Since you're contributing £30 per week (which is more than the £25.60 per week Child Benefit rate for an only child), and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, **you would be eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

Let me provide you with the formal assessment:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news! You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's why:
- Alex is 8 years old, which meets the age requirement (under 16)
- You live in the UK
- Even though Alex doesn't live with you, you're contributing £30 per week towards their upkeep, which is more than the Child Benefit rate of £25.60 per week
- The other parent is not claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and only one person can claim per child

**Important reminder:** If you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000, you may have to pay the High Income Child Benefit tax charge, but you can still claim the benefit.

You can proceed with making a claim for Child Benefit for Alex. Is there anything else you'd like to know?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £30.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Claimant contributes £30.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Claimant contributes £30.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old (under 16), so meets the age requirement. The claimant lives in the UK. Although Alex does not live with the claimant, the claimant is contributing £30 per week towards Alex's upkeep (for clothes, food, and other expenses). This exceeds the Child Benefit rate of £25.60 per week for an only child. According to the eligibility rules, you'll usually be responsible for a child if you're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or the equivalent in kind) towards looking after them. The other parent is not claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and only one person can claim for each child. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their 8-year-old child Alex. Although Alex does not live with the claimant, the claimant contributes £30 per week towards Alex's upkeep, which exceeds the Child Benefit threshold. No one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and only one person can claim per child.

The other parent is not claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and only one person can claim for each child. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
